fact: October 23 is exactly 32 days before the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere - that's like counting down from your birthday to Christmas!
In Alaska, the sun sets before 6 PM on this date, but in southern Florida, you can still catch rays until almost 7 PM on the same day.
Squirrels go absolutely bonkers around October 23, burying up to 25 nuts per day to prepare for winter - that's one nut every 30 minutes during daylight!
Cool nights and warm days around October 23 create perfect conditions for trees to stop making green chlorophyll, which is why leaves turn the most brilliant oranges and reds right around now.
October 23 falls in the middle of Scorpio season, when this mysterious water sign is said to make people extra curious about spooky secrets.
Monarch butterflies are typically flying over 2,000 miles south by this date, traveling at speeds up to 25 miles per hour without stopping to ask for directions.
The moon often looks extra huge and orange when it rises around this time of year because of all the dust and particles floating in the autumn air.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, October 23 brings the smell of fresh grass and blooming flowers as spring kicks into high gear.
Bears in cold regions are typically eating up to 20,000 calories per day around this date - that's like gobbling down 40 hamburgers every single day!
October 23 is day number 296 of the year, which means there are exactly 69 days left until New Year's Eve (or 70 in leap years).
Geese flying south create their famous V-formation around this time because the bird in front breaks the wind for everyone else, making the journey 20% easier.
The first frost of the season often hits many northern areas right around October 23, turning spider webs into glittering crystal artwork overnight.
